policy_statement: Develop at least one “Positive Energy Neighbourhood” in the city.
mechanism_description: Developing positive energy neighbourhoods increases district-scale energy efficiency and local clean energy generation, which displaces fossil energy use and reduces emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Built environment energy systems upgrade (efficiency + local renewables) → ↓ fossil electricity/heat demand/supply → ↓ fuel combustion & grid emissions → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Neighbourhood-scale planning, building retrofits/new builds, and district energy system design to achieve net-positive energy performance through deep efficiency, demand management, and local renewable generation (often with storage and smart controls).  
(2) Mediating physical process: Lower final energy demand (reduced heat losses, more efficient equipment) plus substitution of fossil-derived energy with renewable electricity/heat; reduced upstream and on-site combustion of coal/oil/gas.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in CO₂e emissions from buildings and local energy supply (and potentially from the grid if exporting surplus clean electricity).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions from displacing fossil-based electricity/heat with efficiency and locally generated clean energy at the neighbourhood scale.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: District-scale efficiency and clean generation reduce fossil energy consumption/production, directly lowering GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although positive energy neighbourhoods may improve resilience (e.g., through storage/microgrids) and resource efficiency, the defining and largest climate effect across this mechanism is fossil displacement and emissions reduction—i.e., Mitigation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a “positive energy” claim were achieved via accounting-only measures (e.g., buying certificates) or if local generation displaced already low-carbon grid power, mitigation magnitude could vary; however, the intended primary pathway remains emissions reduction via fossil displacement.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy genuinely implementing positive energy neighbourhood development targets efficiency + local clean supply to offset/displace fossil energy use, making mitigation the consistent primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a specific policy is primarily efficiency-led vs renewable-led, includes storage/resilience objectives, or delivers net exports depends on policy details and is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential edge cases include (a) jurisdictions with already near-zero-carbon grids where additional local generation yields smaller incremental GHG reductions, (b) “positive energy” achieved through offsets/RECs rather than physical displacement, and (c) projects emphasizing resilience (islanding microgrids) where adaptation co-benefits are prominent—none of which change the dominant intended mechanism of mitigation via fossil displacement.
key_indicators: "Develop one or more 'Positive Energy Neighbourhoods'"; "The city has at least one Positive Energy Neighbourhood"
co_benefits: Potential air-quality improvements, lower energy bills/energy poverty reduction, grid resilience and local reliability, innovation/demonstration and green jobs
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be implemented via infrastructure investment (district energy/renewables) or a target-setting frame, but the text itself is framed as a development action/plan without specifying funding or enforceable performance requirements.
